condition.qualitative_description: More than 70% of mean cropped area is under single kharif, with low double-cropped area, indicating dominant rainfed dependence and minimal irrigation access.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In coastal sub-humid zones such as Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh coasts, large proportions of single-kharif cropping relative to double-cropped area directly indicate that farmers rely on monsoon rainfall rather than irrigation. Rainfed areas in South Asia — covering 68% of India's cultivated area — are characterised by single-season cropping and high vulnerability to erratic monsoons. A ratio above 0.70 confirms that irrigation infrastructure is insufficient to support post-monsoon cropping.

condition.qualitative_description: Mean cropping intensity at or below 1.15 with a flat or declining trend confirms single-season rainfed farming with no expansion of irrigated double-cropping over time.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Cropping intensity near 1.0 indicates that nearly all cultivated land is harvested only once per year, consistent with rainfed kharif-only systems. A value above 1.5 would indicate significant irrigation enabling rabi or zaid crops. A flat or declining trend rules out gradual irrigation expansion. In coastal alluvial plains, where groundwater is accessible, persistent low cropping intensity signals that infrastructure or economic barriers prevent irrigation uptake.

condition.qualitative_description: Surface water bodies shrink substantially after kharif (rabi-to-kharif ratio below 0.25) and no irrigation canal is present, confirming absence of surface water storage and conveyance infrastructure for post-monsoon cropping.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Low persistence of surface water bodies into the rabi season indicates that tanks and ponds drain rapidly after the monsoon, leaving no stored water for supplemental irrigation. The absence of a named canal further confirms that farmers have no access to surface irrigation infrastructure. In coastal Odisha and Andhra Pradesh, canal irrigation networks exist in some areas; their absence in a micro-watershed is a strong indicator of rainfed dependency. Together, these signals confirm that water scarcity in rabi and dry spells is structurally driven by lack of infrastructure rather than temporary drought.

condition.qualitative_description: High mean annual runoff (above 300 mm) on mostly plains terrain (cluster 1) indicates significant harvestable water that is currently lost, representing an untapped opportunity for in-situ water harvesting.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In sub-humid coastal zones receiving 900–1400 mm of rainfall (AER-18), a substantial fraction is lost as surface runoff. On plains terrain (cluster 1), this runoff is amenable to capture through farm ponds, bunds, and broad-bed furrows. High runoff relative to rainfall indicates that in-situ water harvesting could significantly reduce rainfed risk, but the current absence of such structures amplifies water scarcity during dry spells. This signal identifies both the severity of the problem and the primary intervention pathway.

condition.qualitative_description: Fewer than 3 MGNREGA irrigation works recorded in the micro-watershed, indicating minimal public investment in irrigation infrastructure.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: MGNREGA irrigation works — including farm ponds, check dams, and field channels — represent the primary public mechanism for expanding irrigation access in rainfed areas. A count below 3 over the programme period indicates that this micro-watershed has not benefited from irrigation infrastructure investment, amplifying its structural rainfed vulnerability. Low MGNREGA irrigation investment also signals an opportunity for targeted intervention rather than a terminal condition.

condition.qualitative_description: Irrigated share of cultivated land is negligible (under roughly 5–10%), confirming structural rainfed dependence; meaningful irrigated area (above roughly 20%) weakens the rainfed-risk diagnosis.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: User-reported irrigated area is the most direct confirmatory variable for rainfed risk when landscape proxies are ambiguous.

confounders.confounder: Meteorological drought causing temporary single-season cropping in otherwise irrigated areas
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Check trend_cropping_intensity and mean_cropping_intensity across multiple years. If cropping intensity was above 1.5 in earlier years but has recently declined, drought rather than structural rainfed dependence is the driver. Also check canal_name — if a canal is present but water delivery has failed, the pathway is irrigation system failure or drought, not rainfed risk. Persistent low cropping intensity across all years (2017–2024) confirms structural rainfed dependence.

confounders.confounder: Coastal salinity or waterlogging limiting rabi cropping rather than water scarcity
confounders.how_to_distinguish: In coastal alluvial plains, low rabi cropping area and low double-crop area may reflect waterlogging or soil salinity constraints rather than water scarcity. Ask farmers whether rabi fields are avoided due to standing water or salt damage. If swb_rabi_area_ha is high (indicating water availability) but lulc_double_crop_ha remains low, salinity or waterlogging is the more likely constraint. Rainfed risk is confirmed when both surface water persistence and canal access are absent.

missing_variable_questions.question_to_user: Approximately what share of your village's farmland is irrigated during the rabi (winter) or zaid (summer) season — would you say less than 10%, between 10–30%, or more than 30%? What are the main irrigation sources used (wells, canals, tanks, ponds)?
missing_variable_questions.how_answer_updates_diagnosis: If less than 10% of farmland is irrigated and sources are limited to seasonal tanks or rain-fed ponds, this strongly confirms the rainfed risk pathway. If more than 30% is irrigated from wells or canals, the water scarcity stress may be better explained by groundwater depletion or canal failure pathways rather than structural rainfed dependence.

choices.label: Less than 10% of cultivated land is irrigated
Between 10% and 30% of cultivated land is irrigated
More than 30% of cultivated land is irrigated

sources.title: Rainfed Areas and Animal Agriculture in Asia: The Wanting Agenda for Transforming Productivity Growth and Rural Poverty
sources.relevance_note: Documents that rainfed areas in India occupy 68% of cultivated area and are characterised by variable rainfall, poor soil quality, and minimal development attention; supports the structural nature of rainfed vulnerability in sub-humid and semi-arid AEZs.

sources.title: Management of crop water under drought: a review
sources.relevance_note: Reviews unproductive water losses (runoff, evaporation) in tropical rainfed systems and identifies in-situ water harvesting, mulching, and short-duration varieties as key management responses; supports sig_04 rationale and recommended solutions.

sources.title: Water-wise Rice Production
sources.relevance_note: Documents water scarcity constraints in rice-based cropping systems in Asia and the role of water-saving technologies; relevant to coastal Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh rice systems under rainfed conditions.

sources.title: Pilot analysis of global ecosystems: Agroecosystems
sources.relevance_note: Provides global agroecosystem characterisation including rainfed area extent and vulnerability; supports contextualisation of rainfed dependence in sub-humid coastal zones.

overall_reasoning_note: Diagnosis of rainfed risk in coastal sub-humid plains (AER-18) should be confirmed by at least two primary signals: dominant single-kharif cropping share (sig_01), persistently low cropping intensity (sig_02), and absence of surface water persistence plus canal infrastructure (sig_03). High runoff on plains terrain (sig_04) and low MGNREGA irrigation investment (sig_05) amplify the diagnosis and identify intervention opportunities. A single signal is insufficient — low cropping intensity alone may reflect drought or salinity. The strongest confirmation is the combination of high single-kharif dominance, no canal, and low rabi surface water persistence. Always rule out coastal waterlogging and salinity as alternative explanations for low rabi cropping before finalising the rainfed risk pathway.
